cue: How do you say something made you suspicious?

es.text: Ponérsele la mosca detrás de la oreja
es.literal: for the fly to settle behind one's ear
es.meaning: To become suspicious; to sense something is off.
es.example: Cuando pidió pagar en efectivo, se me puso la mosca detrás de la oreja.
es.exampleTranslation: When he asked to pay cash, something felt off.

it.literal: to have the flea in the ear
it.meaning: To have a nagging suspicion planted in one's mind.
it.example: Quando ha chiesto di pagare in contanti mi è venuta la pulce nell'orecchio.
it.exampleTranslation: When he asked to pay cash it put a flea in my ear.

bridge.note: Suspicion arrives as an insect at the ear in both languages — and then they differ on species and on geography. Spanish sets a FLY BEHIND the ear: outside, hovering, something you can almost brush away. Italian puts a FLEA INSIDE it: in there, biting, impossible to ignore. Preposition and creature together decide how badly the doubt itches.

interference.es_to_it: The Italian insect is a flea and it goes IN (nell'orecchio), not behind — and it is normally something that comes to you: mi è venuta la pulce nell'orecchio.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener la pulga en la oreja' is not the Spanish idiom; the fly settles BEHIND the ear.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh here). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Cross-references the other fly idiom banked today; a two-insect themed run is possible.
